title: कुन्ती स्तुति
devanagariText:
॥ श्री कुन्ती स्तुति ॥

कुन्त्युवाच —
नमस्ये पुरुषं त्वाद्यमीश्वरं प्रकृतेः परम्।
अलक्ष्यं सर्वभूतानामन्तर्बहिरवस्थितम्॥ १॥

माया-जवनिकाच्छन्नमज्ञाधोक्षजमव्ययम्।
न लक्ष्यसे मूढदृशा नटो नाट्यधरो यथा॥ २॥

तथा परमहंसानां मुनीनाममलात्मनाम्।
भक्तियोगविधानार्थं कथं पश्येम हि स्त्रियः॥ ३॥

कृष्णाय वासुदेवाय देवकीनन्दनाय च।
नन्दगोपकुमाराय गोविन्दाय नमो नमः॥ ४॥

नमः पङ्कजनाभाय नमः पङ्कजमालिने।
नमः पङ्कजनेत्राय नमस्ते पङ्कजाङ्घ्रये॥ ५॥

यथा हृषीकेश खलेन देवकी
कंसेन रुद्धातिचिरं शुचार्पिता।
विमोचिताहं च सहात्मजा विभो
त्वयैव नाथेन मुहुर्विपद्गणात्॥ ६॥

विषान्महाग्नेः पुरुषाददर्शना-
दसत्सभाया वनवासकृच्छ्रतः।
मृधे मृधेऽनेकमहारथास्त्रतो
द्रौण्यस्त्रतश्चास्म हरेऽभिरक्षिताः॥ ७॥

विपदः सन्तु ताः शश्वत्तत्र तत्र जगद्गुरो।
भवतो दर्शनं यत्स्यादपुनर्भवदर्शनम्॥ ८॥

जन्मैश्वर्यश्रुतश्रीभिरेधमानमदः पुमान्।
नैवार्हत्यभिधातुं वै त्वामकिञ्चनगोचरम्॥ ९॥

नमोऽकिञ्चनवित्ताय निवृत्तगुणवृत्तये।
आत्मारामाय शान्ताय कैवल्यपतये नमः॥ १०॥

मन्ये त्वां कालमीशानमनादिनिधनं विभुम्।
समं चरन्तं सर्वत्र भूतानां यन्मिथः कलिः॥ ११॥

न वेद कश्चिद्भगवंश्चिकीर्षितं
तवेहमानस्य नृणां विडम्बनम्।
न यस्य कश्चिद्दयितोऽस्ति कर्हिचि-
द्द्वेष्यश्च यस्मिन्विषमा मतिर्नृणाम्॥ १२॥

जन्म कर्म च विश्वात्मन्नजस्याकर्तुरात्मनः।
तिर्यङ्नृषिषु यादःसु तदत्यन्तविडम्बनम्॥ १३॥

गोप्याददे त्वयि कृतागसि दाम तावद्
या ते दशाश्रुकलिलाञ्जनसम्भ्रमाक्षम्।
वक्त्रं निनीय भयभावनया स्थितस्य
सा मां विमोहयति भीरपि यद्बिभेति॥ १४॥

केचिदाहुरजं जातं पुण्यश्लोकस्य कीर्तये।
यदोः प्रियस्यान्ववाये मलयस्येव चन्दनम्॥ १५॥

अपरे वसुदेवस्य देवक्यां याचितोऽभ्यगात्।
अजस्त्वमस्यक्षयोऽभयस्तव महद्गुणैः॥ १६॥

भवे भवेऽथ भवत्सु कीर्ति-
मसत्सु सज्जगदीश यथेह।
मा भवेत् ग्रथिता हृदि मे
त्वयि रतिरनवच्छिन्ना॥ १७॥

श्रीकृष्ण कृष्णसख वृष्ण्यृषभावनिध्रु-
ग्राज्ञ्यवनी ध्रुवं ववधमानभूरि-भारम्।
भूमेरभारजनयं परदेवतेति
विद्याम कि नोऽतिरहसि श्रीमतः॥ १८॥

॥ इति श्रीमद्भागवतपुराणे प्रथमस्कन्धे कुन्ती स्तुतिः सम्पूर्णा ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Kunti Stuti ||

Kunty-uvacha —
Namasye purusham tvadyam-ishvaram prakriteh param.
Alakshyam sarva-bhutanam-antar-bahir-avasthitam. || 1 ||

Maya-javanika-achchhannam-ajña-adhokshajam-avyayam.
Na lakshyase mudha-drisha nato natya-dharo yatha. || 2 ||

Tatha paramahamsa-nam muninam-amala-atmanam.
Bhakti-yoga-vidhanartham katham pashyema hi striyah. || 3 ||

Krishnaya Vasudevaya Devaki-nandanaya cha.
Nanda-gopa-kumaraya Govindaya namo namah. || 4 ||

Namah pankaja-nabhaya namah pankaja-maline.
Namah pankaja-netraya namas-te pankaja-anghraye. || 5 ||

Yatha Hrishikesha khalena Devaki
Kamsena ruddha-aticiram shuca-arpita.
Vimochita-aham cha saha-atmaja vibho
Tvayaiva nathena muhur-vipad-ganat. || 6 ||

Vishan-maha-agneh purusha-ada-darshana-
Ad-asat-sabhaya vana-vasa-krichchhratah.
Mridhe mridhe-aneka-maha-ratha-astrato
Draunya-astratash-cha-asma hare-abhirakshitah. || 7 ||

Vipadah santu tah shashvat-tatra tatra jagad-guro.
Bhavato darshanam yat-syad-apunar-bhava-darshanam. || 8 ||

Janma-aishvarya-shruta-shribhir-edhamana-madah puman.
Naiva-arhaty-abhidhatum vai tvam-akinchana-gocharam. || 9 ||

Namo-akinchana-vittaya nibritta-guna-vrittaye.
Atma-aramaya shantaya kaivalya-pataye namah. || 10 ||

Manye tvam kalam-ishanam-anadi-nidhanam vibhum.
Samam charantam sarvatra bhutanam yan-mithah kalih. || 11 ||

Na veda kashchid-bhagavamsh-chikirsitam
Taveha-manasya nrinam vidambanam.
Na yasya kashchid-dayito-asti karhichid-
Dveshyash-cha yasmin vishama matir-nrinam. || 12 ||

Janma karma cha vishvatmann-ajasya-akartuh-atmanah.
Tiryang-nri-shishu yadahsu tad-atyanta-vidambanam. || 13 ||

Gopy-adade tvayi krita-agasi dama tavad
Ya te dasha-ashru-kalila-anjana-sambhrama-aksham.
Vaktram niniya bhaya-bhavanaya sthitasya
Sa mam vimohayati bhir-api yad-bibheti. || 14 ||

Kechid-ahur-ajam jatam punya-shlokasya kirtaye.
Yadoh priyasya-anvavaye Malasyeva chandanam. || 15 ||

Apare Vasudevasya Devakyam yachito-abhyagat.
Ajas-tvam-asy-akshayo-abhayas-tava mahad-gunaih. || 16 ||

Bhave bhave-atha bhavatsu kirti-
Masatsu sajjagadisha yatheha.
Ma bhavet grathita hridi me
Tvayi ratir-anavachchhinna. || 17 ||

Shri-Krishna Krishna-sakha Vrishny-rishabha-avani-dhrug-
Rajnya-avani dhruvam vavadhamana-bhuri-bharam.
Bhumer-abhara-janayam para-devata-iti
Vidyama ki no-atirahasi shrimatah. || 18 ||

|| Iti Shrimad Bhagavata Purane Prathama-Skandhe Kunti Stutih Sampurna ||
hindiMeaning:
कुन्ती ने कहा — हे कृष्ण! मैं उन आदि पुरुष को नमस्कार करती हूँ जो ईश्वर हैं, प्रकृति से परे हैं, सब प्राणियों के अन्दर-बाहर स्थित होकर भी अलक्ष्य हैं॥ १॥

माया रूपी पर्दे से ढके हुए, अज्ञानियों की दृष्टि से अगोचर, अव्यय अधोक्षज! आप वैसे ही दिखाई नहीं देते जैसे नाटक में अभिनय करता हुआ नट॥ २॥

कृष्ण को, वासुदेव को, देवकी-नन्दन को, नन्दगोप-कुमार को, गोविन्द को बारम्बार नमस्कार॥ ४॥

कमलनाभ को नमस्कार, कमलमाली को नमस्कार, कमलनेत्र को नमस्कार, कमलचरण को नमस्कार॥ ५॥

हे जगद्गुरो! वे विपत्तियाँ बार-बार आएँ, क्योंकि उन विपत्तियों में आपके दर्शन होते हैं, जो पुनर्जन्म से मुक्ति देने वाले हैं॥ ८॥

जन्म, ऐश्वर्य, विद्या और श्री (सम्पत्ति) से जिसका मद बढ़ रहा है, वह पुरुष आपको सम्बोधित करने योग्य नहीं है, क्योंकि आप अकिञ्चनों (निर्धनों) के ही गोचर हैं॥ ९॥

अकिञ्चनों के धन, गुणातीत, आत्माराम, शान्त, कैवल्यपति को नमस्कार॥ १०॥

मैं जानती हूँ कि आप काल हैं, ईशान हैं, अनादि, अनन्त, विभु हैं। सब प्राणियों में समान विचरण करते हैं, यद्यपि प्राणियों में परस्पर कलह है॥ ११॥
benefits: Teaches the art of total surrender to God in adversity
Invokes Krishna's protection during all kinds of dangers
Invokes the vision that calamities bring divine proximity
Develops vairagya (detachment) from worldly wealth and status
One of the most profound prayers in the Bhagavatam
Invokes the blessing of unbroken devotion (anavachchhinna rati) to the lord
description: Among the most cherished prayers to Lord Krishna, Kunti Stuti (कुन्ती स्तुति) stands as a devotional hymn of praise. This sacred composition is sourced from Shrimad Bhagavata Purana, Canto 1, Chapter 8 and continues to inspire devotees worldwide. Regular recitation is believed to grant the vision that calamities bring divine proximity, invoke Krishna's protection during all kinds of dangers, and teach the art of total surrender to God in adversity. This sacred text is ideally recited on Wednesday (Budhvar) and Thursday (Guruvar) and during Janmashtami and Ekadashi for enhanced blessings. With 18 verses and a reading time of about 12 minutes, this prayer is ideal for both daily worship and special occasions. On this page, you can read the complete Kunti Stuti in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Kunti Stuti from Bhagavata Purana Canto 1 in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning. Queen Kunti's famous prayer to Lord Krishna seeking devotion in adversity.
relatedSearches: Kunti Stuti Srimad Bhagavatam Canto 1 Chapter 8
Kunti prayers to Krishna full Sanskrit text
Vipadah santu nah shashvat meaning explanation
Why did Kunti ask for calamities from Krishna
Kunti Stuti recitation benefits and significance
Bhagavatam Skandha 1 prayers by Kunti and Bhishma

seoFaqs.question: Where in the Bhagavata Purana does the Kunti Stuti appear?
seoFaqs.answer: The Kunti Stuti is found in the Srimad Bhagavata Purana, Skandha (Canto) 1, Chapter 8, verses 18 to 43. It is spoken by Queen Kunti — mother of the Pandavas and aunt of Krishna — at the moment when Krishna is preparing to leave Hastinapura after the Mahabharata war and return to Dvaraka. The stuti consists of approximately twenty-six Sanskrit verses (Adhyatma Shloka tradition counts vary slightly) and is regarded as one of the most theologically rich prayers in the entire Bhagavata. The narration is given by Suta Goswami to the sages assembled at Naimisharanya. (Sources: A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada Bhagavatam 1.8; sanskritdocuments.org Kunti-stuti.)

seoFaqs.question: Why does Queen Kunti ask Krishna for calamities rather than comforts?
seoFaqs.answer: In verse 8 (vipadah santu nah shashvat tatra tatra jagad-guro), Kunti famously prays that calamities may keep arising in her life so that she may remember Krishna constantly, since the vision of the Lord obtained in the midst of suffering grants liberation from rebirth (apunar-bhava-darshanam). Her insight is that material prosperity tends to make one forgetful of God, while distress keeps the heart turned toward divine refuge. This single verse has become a cornerstone of bhakti theology and is quoted by acharyas to illustrate that adversity, when accepted as Krishna's mercy, becomes a vehicle for the highest spiritual realization. (Sources: Prabhupada Bhagavatam 1.8.25 purport; Sanatana Goswami Brihad-Bhagavatamrita.)

seoFaqs.question: What dangers had the Pandavas survived through Krishna's protection?
seoFaqs.answer: In verses 6 and 7, Kunti recounts the long list of perils from which Krishna shielded her family: the poisoned food prepared by Duryodhana for Bhima, the fire trap of the lacquer house at Varanavata, the cannibal demon Hidimba, the wicked assembly where Draupadi was disrobed, the hardships of the twelve-year forest exile, the weapons of countless great warriors on the Kurukshetra battlefield, and finally the brahmastra of Ashvatthama that targeted the womb of Uttara to kill the unborn Parikshit. Each of these crises had been resolved by Krishna's personal intervention, and Kunti's prayer is essentially a grateful acknowledgement of his unfailing protection. (Sources: Mahabharata Adi-Sabha-Vana Parvas; Bhagavatam 1.8 narrative context.)

seoFaqs.question: What does Kunti mean by calling Krishna akinchana-gocharam?
seoFaqs.answer: In verse 9 and 10 Kunti makes the radical theological statement that those puffed up by janma (birth), aishvarya (wealth), shruta (learning) and shri (beauty) cannot truly address Krishna, because the Lord is akinchana-gocharah — accessible only to those who have nothing, to the destitute and the surrendered. She salutes him as akinchana-vittaya, the wealth of those who own nothing, and as kaivalya-pati, the lord of liberation. This passage is the scriptural basis for the bhakti principle that humility and absence of false pride are the prerequisites for receiving Krishna's mercy, and is frequently cited in Vaishnava sermons on the spiritual qualifications for devotion. (Sources: Bhagavatam 1.8.26; Madhva and Vallabha bhashyas.)

seoFaqs.question: Why is Kunti Stuti considered such an important prayer in the Bhagavata?
seoFaqs.answer: Acharyas treat Kunti Stuti as the first complete kirtana-stuti of the Bhagavata Purana — the first place where the philosophical heart of the text is articulated by a devotee in direct prayer. It contains in compact form virtually every major theme of bhakti yoga: Krishna's transcendence above maya, his hiddenness from worldly eyes (mudha-drisha), his playful descent for the protection of the devotees, his equal vision over all beings (samam charantam sarvatra), his special accessibility to the akinchana, and the prayer for unbroken devotion (anavachchhinna rati). For this reason it is included in the daily parayana of many Vaishnava sampradayas. (Sources: Prabhupada Teachings of Queen Kunti book; Bhagavata Mahatmya.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the famous verse about Krishna being bound by mother Yashoda?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 14 (gopy-adade tvayi krita-agasi dama) is Kunti's wonder-filled meditation on the Damodara-lila — the episode in which the gopi Yashoda, having caught child Krishna stealing butter, tied him to a wooden mortar with a rope. Kunti marvels that the same Krishna whom even fear itself fears, who is the all-pervading Supreme, allowed his mother to bind him out of pure parental love, his lotus eyes filled with tears and his face lowered in mock shame. This verse has inspired countless Vaishnava paintings, songs and the entire Damodarashtaka recited during Kartik month, and is considered one of the most tender devotional passages in all of Sanskrit literature. (Sources: Bhagavatam 10.9 Damodara-lila; Satyavrata Muni Damodarashtaka.)

seoFaqs.question: When and how is Kunti Stuti traditionally recited?
seoFaqs.answer: Kunti Stuti is recited daily by many Vaishnava devotees as part of their Bhagavata-parayana, and is given special emphasis during the month of Kartik, on Ekadashi tithis, on Janmashtami, and during the seven-day Bhagavata Saptaha. Some Gaudiya Vaishnava households recite it before meals as a reminder of Krishna's protection in adversity. The recitation is typically done seated facing east, with the Bhagavata text on a wooden stand, after a brief invocation of Suta Goswami and the listening sages of Naimisharanya. ISKCON has popularised it worldwide through Prabhupada's small book Teachings of Queen Kunti, which provides an accessible English translation and verse-by-verse commentary. (Sources: ISKCON daily sadhana guides; traditional Bhagavata-parayana paddhati.)
